dashboard.greetingSub: You're on track. GS3 Economy session pending for today.

currentAffairs.heroSub: 7 stories • UPSC-mapped • GS1 to GS4 + Essay
currentAffairs.news1Title: RBI keeps repo rate unchanged at 6.5% — signals wait-and-watch stance
currentAffairs.news1Desc: The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) unanimously decided to keep the repo rate at 6.5% for the eighth consecutive time, signalling continued focus on bringing inflation durably below the 4% target before considering rate cuts. Governor Shaktikanta Das noted that while food inflation is easing, core inflation remains sticky.
currentAffairs.news1Links: GS3 — Monetary Policy tools (Repo, CRR, SLR, OMO) · Inflation targeting framework · MPC composition & independence · GS2 — RBI as a Constitutional/Statutory body · Central bank autonomy debate
currentAffairs.news2Title: India-Maldives relations: Foreign Minister visits New Delhi, signs 3 MoUs
currentAffairs.news2Desc: Maldivian Foreign Minister Moosa Zameer's New Delhi visit signals a diplomatic reset after months of tension following President Muizzu's pro-China pivot. India has extended a financial support package and signed agreements in health, digital infrastructure, and defence cooperation.
currentAffairs.news2Links: GS2 — India's Neighbourhood First Policy · SAARC relevance · Indian Ocean Region geopolitics · GS3 — Blue Economy · Essay — India as a regional power
currentAffairs.news3Title: Forest Rights Act — SC upholds community forest rights over diversion
currentAffairs.news3Desc: The Supreme Court reinforced that Forest Rights Act, 2006 gram sabha consent is mandatory before any forest diversion for development projects. The ruling has implications for several pending infrastructure projects in tribal regions.
currentAffairs.news3Links: GS1 — Tribal communities & culture · GS2 — 5th & 6th Schedule · PESA Act · Gram Sabha powers · GS3 — FRA vs Development conflict · GS4 — Environmental ethics

studyContent.title: Study Content
studyContent.subtitle: Syllabus-mapped notes • PYQ-linked • Multiple formats
studyContent.q1Body1: Fiscal policy refers to the use of government revenue collection (taxation) and expenditure to influence a nation's economy. In India, the Union Budget, presented annually on February 1st, is the primary instrument of fiscal policy. The Ministry of Finance, through the Budget Division, prepares it.
studyContent.q1Body2: Unlike monetary policy (controlled by RBI), fiscal policy is controlled by the elected government — making it inherently political as well as economic. This distinction is important for UPSC questions on institutional autonomy.
studyContent.pyqQuestion1: "Discuss the role of fiscal policy in India's post-pandemic economic recovery. What structural reforms should be prioritised to achieve sustainable growth?"
studyContent.bullet1: Expenditure Policy: Capital expenditure (productive, creates assets) vs. Revenue expenditure (subsidies, salaries). India's capex push — ₹11.11 lakh crore in Union Budget 2024-25.
studyContent.bullet2: Deficit Financing: When expenditure exceeds revenue. Fiscal Deficit target: 5.1% of GDP (2024-25). FRBM Act 2003 mandates deficit reduction path.
studyContent.pyqQuestion2: "The FRBM Act has failed to achieve its stated objectives. Do you agree? Analyse."
studyContent.dataBull2: Capital Expenditure: ₹11.11 lakh crore (2024-25 Budget) — 3x of 2019-20

prelimsLab.title: Prelims Practice Lab
prelimsLab.subtitle: 1,240 solved · 71% accuracy · Economy & S&T are weak areas
prelimsLab.qQuestion: With reference to the Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) of India, consider the following statements:
prelimsLab.qS1: 1. It consists of six members — three from RBI and three external experts nominated by the Central Government.
prelimsLab.qS2: 2. Decisions are taken by majority vote, and the Governor has a casting vote in case of a tie.
prelimsLab.qS3: 3. It meets at least four times a year as mandated by the RBI Act.
prelimsLab.qPrompt: Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsLab.insightText: Economy accuracy is 58% — below cutoff safety threshold. Next session will serve 60% Economy questions to close the gap.

community.title: Community
community.subtitle: 4,280 active aspirants · Doubt-clear, peer review, study groups
community.post1Title: Difference between Repo Rate and Reverse Repo Rate in policy transmission?
community.post1Desc: I understand the definitions, but struggle to explain how a 25bp repo rate cut actually transmits to home loan EMIs in practice. Can someone explain the step-by-step mechanism with real numbers? The RBI-bank-customer chain is confusing me.
community.post2Title: My GS2 Federalism answer got 21/25 in peer review — sharing what worked
community.post2Desc: Peer review was invaluable. The key feedback: I was listing facts but not "critically examining." The structure that worked: Context para → Issue from each dimension (legislative/executive/financial) → SC judgements → Way forward with specific recommendations. Posting my answer in comments.

wellbeing.title: Wellbeing Centre
wellbeing.subtitle: Your Wellbeing Centre
wellbeing.desc: UPSC is a long game. Sustainable study beats burnout every time.
wellbeing.moodSub: Takes 10 seconds. We'll adjust your plan if needed.
wellbeing.moodAlert: Great! You're feeling good. Full plan active for today. Keep this energy going.
wellbeing.insightText: You're studying efficiently. Average session length is 87 minutes — healthy for retention. Wednesday was your most productive day (4.2 hrs). Consider similar schedule Fridays.
wellbeing.slowDesc: Having a difficult week? Activate this — we reduce your daily targets by 40% for 5 days, no guilt. UPSC is a marathon.
wellbeing.storiesTitle: Real Stories — Because You Need to Hear These
wellbeing.story1Text: "Failed 4 times. My family stopped asking about it. I started treating each failure as data, not shame. AIR 89 in my 5th attempt."
wellbeing.story2Text: "I was working a full-time job. 3 hours a day, 6 days a week. The difference was consistency over intensity. No 14-hour days."
wellbeing.story3Text: "Answer writing was terrifying at first. I wrote 200 answers before Mains. The fear went away. The scores didn't matter — the practice did."
